translation: If the purchase price is paid by a child, it shall be examined whether the child has actual purchasing capacity. A minor child without independent economic resources generally lacks purchasing capacity. In such cases, the nature of the property under the minor child’s name should be discussed as a key focus. As a member of the family, the property under the minor child’s name, regardless of whether it originates from either parent, generally constitutes family common property. However, it does not exclude the possibility that certain minor children indeed possess personal property. From the perspective of protecting the obligee’s legitimate rights and interests and maintaining transaction security, such property shall undoubtedly be deemed as belonging to the minor child. When handling enforcement cases, courts shall not include such property within the scope of enforcement, nor shall they take compulsory measures such as freezing or sealing up against it.
